gaps: Long-range historical stitching beyond the native 5-year daily cap (overlapping-window rescaling) is stubbed, not built — deferred to v2 unless explicitly approved as shipping scope.

novel_features.name: Time-Travel Trending Lookup
novel_features.description: See what was trending on a specific past date and geo — a question Google Trends' own live UI cannot answer.
novel_features.rationale: Queries the local trending_topic table populated by every prior sync; the live site only ever exposes the current window.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this when reconstructing what was trending on a specific past day instead of scraping the live site, which cannot show historical trending snapshots at all.

novel_features.name: Trend History Search
novel_features.description: Full-text search across every related-term and trending-topic result you've ever synced, offline.
novel_features.rationale: Runs a SQLite FTS5 query over locally cached data with no live API call — no other tool retains this history at all.
novel_features.example: google-trends-pp-cli trends history search "electric vehicle" --table related --agent
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use this to check whether a term you tracked weeks ago ever surfaced as related or trending, without re-querying the rate-limited live API.

novel_features.name: Snapshot Diff / Rising-Term Watchlist
novel_features.description: See exactly what changed for a keyword's related terms and interest score since your last sync.
novel_features.rationale: Diffs two dated local snapshots of the same keyword in SQLite — nothing to diff against without a persisted history.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this instead of re-eyeballing a chart when you need to know precisely what's new since last week.

novel_features.name: Content Opportunity Ranking
novel_features.description: Ranks a keyword's related/rising terms by rising-momentum times parent-topic growth, surfacing the best content ideas first.
novel_features.rationale: Joins related_term rising values against interest_over_time trend slope, both already in local SQLite — a mechanical ranking, not a raw list.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use this when you need a shortlist of content ideas ranked by momentum, not the raw related-queries dump.

novel_features.name: Seasonality Index
novel_features.description: Computes monthly averages, peak month, and a seasonality strength score for a keyword from cached history.
novel_features.rationale: Pure computation over already-synced interest_over_time rows — no external call, no dependency on long-range stitching.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this to detect a keyword's seasonal pattern from cached history instead of eyeballing a chart by hand.

novel_features.name: Stale Query Report
novel_features.description: Lists tracked keywords whose local data hasn't been refreshed recently, so you know what to re-sync before you hit a rate limit.
novel_features.rationale: Reads last-synced timestamps from local SQLite — directly mitigates this API's confirmed aggressive, unpredictable 429 throttling.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Check this before a bulk re-sync so you refresh only what's actually stale, given this API's confirmed rate-limit fragility.

novel_features.name: Geo-Divergence Finder
novel_features.description: Ranks the regions where two keywords' interest diverges most, instead of raw per-region values you'd have to compare by hand.
novel_features.rationale: Calls comparedgeo for two keywords in one request, then computes and ranks the delta locally — a synthesis step no single API call provides.
novel_features.example: google-trends-pp-cli trends geo-gap nike adidas --resolution REGION --agent
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use this for a ranked list of the biggest regional gaps between two keywords, not the raw per-region compare.

narrative.headline: Every Google Trends workflow pytrends offered, plus the local history and rate-limit resilience that archived, frozen wrapper never had.
narrative.value_prop: pytrends — the de-facto standard Python wrapper — was archived in April 2025, and Google Trends throttles anonymous access aggressively and unpredictably. This CLI absorbs the full explore-to-widget contract with built-in retry/backoff, caches every query locally so history compounds instead of evaporating, and answers questions the live site simply cannot: what was trending three weeks ago, what changed since your last check, and which related terms are actually worth a content brief.
narrative.auth_narrative: Google Trends has no login and no API key. Plain, unfingerprinted HTTP clients (a bare curl, Go's default net/http) get blocked (HTTP 429) on first contact with the explore/widget API — confirmed live during generation. This CLI's built-in Chrome-TLS-fingerprinted transport gets past that block on its own; no setup step is required. `auth login --chrome` is optional — it imports a clearance cookie from the user's logged-in Chrome session, which can help under sustained heavy-use rate-limiting, but it is not a prerequisite for normal use.

quickstart.comment: Confirms the CLI and local database are set up correctly before you make any live calls.
The most common call: interest-over-time for a single keyword. Works out of the box, no auth step needed.
Syncs related/rising search terms locally — this is what powers the local-only commands below.
A transcendence command: ranks content ideas from what's already synced, no extra API call.

troubleshoots.fix: Google Trends throttles aggressively and inconsistently. Wait a minute and retry — the CLI's built-in adaptive backoff handles routine cases, but sustained blocking may benefit from running `auth login --chrome` for a clearance cookie.

troubleshoots.symptom: `trends trending` fails with "server returned an error frame"
troubleshoots.fix: Known gap, not a transient failure. The Trending Now page's batchexecute RPC is Google's undocumented internal protocol; this CLI sends the same request shape and session tokens a real browser sends, but the server currently returns an error frame instead of trending-terms data. auth login --chrome and retrying will not fix this. Every other trends command is unaffected and confirmed working live.

troubleshoots.symptom: Related/rising query counts look different from the live website
troubleshoots.fix: This is expected — Google's own server-side sampling means identical queries can return different numbers, especially for low-volume terms and small geographies. Not a CLI bug.

narrative.when_to_use: Use this CLI for programmatic Google Trends research: interest-over-time pulls, multi-keyword and cross-geo comparisons, related/rising query discovery for content planning, and tracking trending topics over time via the local cache. It is the right tool whenever you need history Google Trends' own UI discards, or when you're automating what used to be a manual pytrends script.
anti_triggers: Do not use this CLI for absolute search volume — Google Trends only exposes relative 0-100 interest, never real query counts.
Do not use this CLI for real-time (sub-hourly) trend monitoring at scale — the API's aggressive rate limiting makes tight polling loops unreliable.
Do not use this CLI for authenticated Google Ads Keyword Planner-style volume data — that requires a separate Google Ads account and API, not Trends.

recipes.title: Compare a brand against two competitors
recipes.command: google-trends-pp-cli trends interest nike --compare adidas,puma --geo US --timeframe "today 3-m" --json
recipes.explanation: Returns all three keywords on the same 0-100 scale in one call, the shared-scale comparison pytrends required manual payload construction for.

recipes.title: Find this week's best content ideas from a topic
recipes.explanation: Ranks rising related terms by momentum instead of dumping the raw related-queries list.

recipes.title: See what changed since last week without re-scraping
recipes.explanation: Diffs the last two local snapshots — a question the live site cannot answer since it has no history.

recipes.title: Pull a large regional breakdown and narrow to the fields that matter
recipes.command: google-trends-pp-cli trends region coffee --resolution CITY --geo US --agent --select region_name,value
recipes.explanation: City-level breakdowns return dozens of rows with extra metadata; --select trims the response to just the two fields an agent typically needs.

recipes.title: Check what was trending on a specific past date
recipes.explanation: Answers a question the live Trending Now page cannot — it only ever shows the current window.
